coredata.dc:title: Cation order and disorder in cathode materials for Li-ion batteries

coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yue
Huang, Jiaqiang
Li, Biao
coredata.dc:description: Design of cathode materials has been the central topic of Li-batteries since its invention. Beyond chemical composition, another dimension of the material design resides at crystal structures where factors like ionic size, coordination environment, and superstructure play significant roles. In this review, we shift to another focus, i.e. cation order and disorder, that has been prevailing in recent years in the field of cathode materials, to overview how this structural feature emerges to govern the cathode electrochemistry. We begin with a broad conceptualization of cation order and disorder across various scales, followed by an examination of the thermodynamic and kinetic factors that underlie their formation. We then revisit how cation order and disorder evolve along with cycling that is crucial in determining the cycle life of cathode materials. The roles of cation order and disorder on various aspects of electrochemistry, such as Li diffusion, cycling stability, anionic redox activity, voltage profile and voltage hysteresis, are subsequently summarized and discussed. We lastly extend our review to paying attention on the experimental tailoring and characterizing of cation arrangement in cathodes that are pivotal for future cathode design.

head.ce:title: Cation order and disorder in cathode materials for Li-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Design of cathode materials has been the central topic of Li-batteries since its invention. Beyond chemical composition, another dimension of the material design resides at crystal structures where factors like ionic size, coordination environment, and superstructure play significant roles. In this review, we shift to another focus, i.e. cation order and disorder, that has been prevailing in recent years in the field of cathode materials, to overview how this structural feature emerges to govern the cathode electrochemistry. We begin with a broad conceptualization of cation order and disorder across various scales, followed by an examination of the thermodynamic and kinetic factors that underlie their formation. We then revisit how cation order and disorder evolve along with cycling that is crucial in determining the cycle life of cathode materials. The roles of cation order and disorder on various aspects of electrochemistry, such as Li diffusion, cycling stability, anionic redox activity, voltage profile and voltage hysteresis, are subsequently summarized and discussed. We lastly extend our review to paying attention on the experimental tailoring and characterizing of cation arrangement in cathodes that are pivotal for future cathode design.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-based battery materials consist of a wide range of crystallized structures in which Li+ can stay, diffuse, or react with to form new phases. The electrochemical properties of battery materials are highly related to the crystal structures that determines the Li site energies, Li diffusivity as well as redox processes [1–4]. Therefore, exploring the fundamental relationship between crystallography and Li-driven electrochemical performance has been the long-lasting topic for battery materials, and especially for cathodes wherein topotactic reactions involving Li+, that is, lithium insertion and extraction, prevail. After a journey of several decades, a lot of structures have been found capable of reversible Li insertion and extraction, but only few of them were shown promising in terms of battery applications [5–10]. These well-ordered structures, some of them known as layered rock-salt, spinel, olivine, form the foundation of nowadays Li-ion batteries.

The optimal crystallographic ordering between Li and transition metal (TM) ions, as represented by layered ordering between Li and TM, allows Li diffusivity and hence the reversibility of cycling. However, Li-ion hosts have to go through metastable states, that is, thermodynamically unstable states upon lithium removal. A typical example is the end member of LiCoO2 during delithiation, namely, CoO2, which is metastable and can barely survive during long cycling [11,12]. Thermodynamically driven structural changes, such as TM ion migration, phase transition and oxygen release, are detrimental to the reversibility, leading to the poor cycling performance for LiCoO2 when charging to high voltage [13–15]. This undesirable order-to-disorder structural transition is common in layered rock-salt structures, and tremendous efforts have been dedicated to understanding and inhibiting such disordering, especially those induced by TM migration [16,17].

Although cation disorder is chronically believed to be detrimental to electrochemical performance, a recent arising concept of cation-disordered rock-salt structure challenges our conventional belief once more Li ions are put inside, which boosts the Li diffusivity via opening additional percolation pathway, thereby delivering comparable capacity to layered Li-rich cathodes [18]. This new class of materials, known as “DRX” cathodes, are presumed to have better structural stability than layered ones since their pre-existing disordered structure is immune to order-to-disorder transition. But in real cases, cycling performance of DRX compounds are far from reaching their practicability, which suffers from severe voltage fade and capacity fade [19–21]. More studies are pending for revealing the structural evolutions along cycling.

The above two typical cases (structural disordering in layered cathodes and DRX cathodes) are good examples showing how cation order and disorder matter in governing battery performance. In a broader sense, cation order and disorder should coexist together, while they differ in different length scales. A structure with long-range ordering, such as LiNi1/3Co1/3Mn1/3O2, should also have disordering of cations locally as Ni, Co and Mn may occupy randomly in transition metal layer [22]. Besides, full cation-disordered compounds were also shown to have cation ordering in a local range, which is crucial in determining the Li diffusivity via percolations [4]. Therefore, defining cation order and disorder needs to specify length scales, but how long- and short-range (dis)orderings contribute to different performance metrics deserves further explorations.

Given these importances of cation order and disorder, a general review with clearer definition and classification of such scale-dependent cation (dis)order as well as their structure-property relationship, is highly desired. This motivated us to summarize previous works and recent progresses on cation order and disorder to give a clearer picture of these different structural features and how they determine electronic structures and performances, thereby guiding the design of future high-energy-density cathode materials. The review will start with a general description of concepts by understanding how thermodynamics and kinetics determines the cation arrangement. Then, we summarize the TM-migration induced cation ordering and disordering during Li-driven reaction, including order-to-disorder and disorder-to-order transitions. The short-range-ordering will be specially discussed as a section to reveal the scale-dependence of cation (dis)ordering. After this, we disclose how the cation ordering impacts on the electrochemical performances including cycling, potential, voltage hysteresis and decay, and rate performance. Finally, the synthetic and characterization techniques for creating and differentiating cation order and disorder are also summarized. To end this review, we deliver our perspectives on how to leverage such cation (dis)ordering to guide further cathode materials design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General conceptualization of cation order and disorder
ce:section.ce:para: To start with, we firstly briefly define and classify general cation order and disorder concepts in relevant cathode materials and how they are formed based on thermodynamic and kinetic considerations. Most of the cathodes in lithium ion batteries are preferred as well-ordered materials in which lithium and other transition metal (TM) ions occupy different crystallographic sites [23], such as layered LiCoO2 wherein the Li and Co sit at 3a and 3b Wyckoff positions respectively, as shown schematically in Fig. 1a. Nevertheless, in DRX cathode materials, such as α-LiFeO2 (Fig. 1b) and Li1.17Ti0.58Ni0.25O2 [3], lithium and transition metal ions are randomly distributed on cationic sites without discrimination. In pure mathematical considerations, the probabilities of one specific cationic site being occupied by each cation are determined by their stoichiometric proportions. For example, Li:Fe =1:1 in α-LiFeO2 leads to a 50 % probability of one specific cationic site to be occupied by lithium and 50 % probability to be occupied by iron. However, it is not that simple in real cases which necessitate thermodynamic and even kinetic considerations in describing a structure. For instance, in some systems it requires the cations to be distributed as homogeneous as possible for achieving a lowest free energy while in some systems clustering of cations could exist because of metastability.

Disorder can be present in ordered materials, typical cases being the disordered arrangement of Ni, Mn, and Co in the transition metal layer and Li-Ni mixing in lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC, chemical formula: LiNixCoyMn1-x-yO2) cathodes (Fig. 1c) as well as Li-Fe mixing in LiFePO4 [24–27]. Disordered materials also show orderings in a short-range scale, called as short-range ordering (SRO), which refers to the regular and predictable arrangement (i.e. crystalline lattice) of atoms over a short distance, usually within one or two atom spacings [28], such as the two types of octahedral clusters in α-LiFeO2 [29] as illustrated in Fig. 1d. Given those cases where cation ordering and disordering are highly related with each other, understanding the order-disorder characteristics is of great significance to design new cathodes with desired electrochemical performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Origins of cation order and disorder
ce:section.ce:para: How cations are arranged in oxygen framework generally is a result of the delicate competition between the thermodynamics and kinetics of the structure. For thermodynamic reasons, there are numerous factors governing the cation order or disorder in material science. One of the most prominent factors causing cation disorder is the similarity in ionic radius. According to Hume-Rothery rule, which was proposed in the 1920s within the realm of metal alloy research, solid solution is prone to form when ionic radii difference is less than 15 %[30,31]. Indeed, the cationic ordering in typical layered oxide cathodes in lithium-ion batteries highly obeys this rule. This is also corroborated theoretically by Wu et al. [32]. through modelling Buckingham interaction potential of AMO2 (A = alkali metal; M = transition metal). For a composition of LiMO2, Li+ and M3+ prefer to mix to form a cation-disordered cubic rock-salt compound ( Fig. 2a) if M3+ is Fe3+ (high-spin) or Mn3+ (high-spin) whose ionic radius is around 0.645 Å, which is close to that of Li+ (0.76 Å). However, for Co3+ that has a relatively lower ionic radius of 0.545 Å, LiCoO2 tends to form a layered structure wherein Li and Co separately form layers in oxygen interlayer spacings (See Fig. 2b and Fig. 3a for more LiMO2 structures with different M3+ radius).

Further moving from LiMO2 to Li2MO3, higher valence of M in Li2MO3 (M4+) has a smaller radius than that of M in LiMO2, which deviates more from the size of Li+ and thus favor a more ordered structure. This helps explain why LiMnO2 is cation disordered but Li2MO3 has an ordered structure with honeycomb ordering in TM layer (Fig. 2c). Such Li+ and Mn4+ ordering is not limited to these cases as the large family of rock-salt oxides enables a diverse range of lithium-based compounds, typical examples being Li3NbO4 and Li4MoO5, for which the lithium and TM ions in TM layer are in different orderings. In addition to the radius effect, charge effect is another factor that determines whether LiMO2 is a disordered or ordered structure. Based on Wu’s modeling result [32], large charge difference between Li+ and transition metal ion favors disordered α-LiFeO2 type structure while similar charge promotes the formation of layered α-NaFeO2 type structure. But the influence of charge effect is less significant compared with that of ionic radii. The similarity between the radius of cations is determinative, in most of the cases, to the formation of cation disorder. Early archetypical cases were frequently reported in perovskites, in which similar ionic radius of the B’ and B’’ species induced B-site cation disorder in A(B’ B’’)O3 compounds[33]. In battery community, similar radius of Ni2+ and Li+ contributes to the well-known Li-Ni mixing in layered oxide cathodes, such as NMC (LiNixCoyMn1-x-yO2,1<x, y, z<1), Lithium-rich NMC (LR-NMC, Li[LiδNixCoyMn1-δ-x-y]O2, 1<δ, x, y<1) …, somewhat influencing the electrochemical behavior of these materials. Li-Ni mixing in cathode materials results in reduced Li-ion layer space, thus hampering Li-ion diffusion. Besides, it also generates significant anisotropic stress and impacts the structural stability of NMC materials, leading to voltage and capacity fading in the battery [25]. However, some researchers also proposed the positive effect of a small amount of Li-Ni mixing on thermal stability and cycling stability due to the presence of stable 180° Ni-O-Ni super-exchange chain in Ni-rich NMCs, which will be further explained in the following part [6,19,34,35].

According to radius effect, Li-Ni mixing should be decreased if there were much more Ni3+ than Ni2+ in NMC material due to much larger difference in ionic radius between Ni3+ and Li+ compared to that of Ni2+ and Li+. However, Li-Ni mixing was still observed severe in Ni-rich NMC material where Ni2+ is lessened due to the decreased amount of Mn4+ [24,25,36,37]. To reconcile this puzzle, magnetic frustration and super-exchange effect were proposed [38,39]. In TM layer of NMC material, three triangularly placed Ni2+ or Ni3+ cations will always have two opposing magnetic moments due to antiferromagnetic (AF) interaction between Ni ions, creating “magnetic frustration”. Because Li+ does not have magnetic moment, Li+ in Ni position will relieve this magnetic frustration, which is supposed to be one of the thermodynamic origins of Li-Ni disorder (Fig. 3b). Besides, after the exchange of Li and Ni, the antisite Ni2+, which has a magnetic moment opposite to the Ni ions in the transition metal (TM) layer, readily forms 180° (linear) super-exchanges with cations in the TM layer through the σ bond created between the eg orbital of the Ni2+ and the p orbital of O2- (Fig. 3c). In other words, the opposite magnetic moments of the antisite Ni2+ and the Ni ions in the TM layer allow them to easily interact and exchange electrons through a strong chemical bond involving the eg of Ni2+and p orbitals of O2-, which lowers total energy of the system. Non-magnetic Co3+ ions in a low spin state doping will relieve Li-Ni mixing because it alleviates magnetic frustration in transition metal layer and weakens super-exchange effect. Mn4+ will exacerbate Li-Ni mixing because it not only enables the formation of Ni2+ due to charge compensation, but also intensifies magnetic frustration and super-exchange effect [25]. According to the DFT calculation of Zheng’s group [39], Ni3+ has the priority to exchange with Li and changes to a Ni2+ state with a spin-flip and the addition of an electron from the nearest Co (Co3+), which is further oxidized from Co3+ to Co4+ due to the charge compensation after Ni3+/Li+ exchange [39]. Thus, such magnetic frustration and super exchange effect helps explain why Li-Ni mixing still massively presents in nickel rich NMCs, but their validity needs further scrutiny.

Regardless of whether it is true or not, the theories of magnetic frustration and super exchange bring new insights to account for the cation disorder in addition to the classical belief of size and charge effect. However, to decouple which factors play the predominant role in causing the cation disorder requires further studies and would be a difficult task. For example, Wen et al. [40]. reduced Ni2+ content in Li1+xNi1-xO2 by increasing Li+ content and successfully suppressed Li-Ni mixing. They ascribed this phenomenon to decreased Ni2+ content, which has similar radius with Li+. But this may also be caused by released magnetic frustration due to the presence of Li+ in transition metal layer. Besides, it has long been believed that Mn4+ doping aggravates Li-mixing by increasing Ni2+ content [26]. But it exacerbates magnetic frustration at the same time. Thus, there is still a long way to go to clarify the factors that are causing Li-Ni mixing and help us control the extent of Li-Ni mixing precisely.

Beyond the above proposed understandings, another family of disordered compounds, known as DRX, have quite different rationales behind the origin of cation disorder. Li1.17Ti0.33Fe0.5O2 [2], for example, has a ground-state structure of DRX, even though the radius difference between Li+ and Ti4+ exceeds 15 %, a threshold below which a solid solution can form based on Hume-Rothery rules. By surveying previous DRX materials [41], it is not difficult to find that most DRX materials are based on doping of transition metals without d electrons, such as Ti4+, Nb5+, Mo6+….Much efforts have been devoted to disclosing this phenomenon and help better material design. Wolverton et al. [42]. proved that the equilibrium temperature at which a structure becomes disorder is determined by the interplay between the entropy (TS) and energy (E) changes associated with the disorder. The increase in entropy due to disorder is not influenced by the specific chemistry involved, but energy increase depends on the system's ability to accommodate that disorder. Thus, it will be more meaningful to investigate energy change to help understand the different material structures with different chemical compositions. If the energy cost associated with disorder is higher, the system will be disordered at a higher temperature and difficult for synthesis.

As it is shown in Fig. 3d, DRX materials generally suffer from structure distortion due to cation mixing; this is not difficult to understand since disordering means disparate local environments of oxygen ions that are surrounded by multiple kinds of cations. This distortion leads to higher system energy and make DRX structure unstable. Based on Density Functional Theory (DFT), Urban et al. [43]. demonstrated that elements with no d electrons (Ti4+, Nb5+, Mo6+…) can accommodate structure distortion at the lowest energy cost. Although they didn’t specify the reason for this phenomenon, this conclusion still helps us understand why materials containing d 0 transition metals are more prone to forming a DRX structure. This has already been demonstrated in tremendous experimental results such as the successful synthesis of Li+/Mo6+ [44–46], Li+/Nb5+ [5] or Li+/Ti4+ [47] based DRX material, although they all have large ionic radius and charge differences.

Despite of the cation ordering/disordering in the pristine state, electrochemical lithiation or delithiation can also alter the thermodynamic and kinetic stability of the structure and thus trigger the cation rearrangement. These are well exemplified by cases where intralayer (inside TM layer) or interlayer (between TM layer and Li layer) cation migration occurs. The reason why such cation migration happens is diverse, but all can be considered as thermodynamically driven consequences. Lithium extraction normally is accompanied by layer spacing change (for layered structure) and charge compensation from redox centers, leading to metastable structures, some of which can hardly maintain the original lattice framework and end up with structural change, such as cation migrations. Understanding these phenomena could benefit the rational design of cathode materials free of cation rearrangement that is generally detrimental to battery performances.

The most frequently observed cases are TM migration from TM layer to Li layer, inducing cation disordering, sometime either called as “Li-TM anti-site”. Such interlayer TM migration is attributed to be the cause of “layer-to-spinel” phase transition in most of the Mn-based layered oxides, such as LiMnO2 [48], lithium and manganese rich (LMR) NMCs[49,50]. The thermodynamic driving force of Mn migration should apparently be the phase instability of a delithiated layered structure where Mn can migrate from transition metal layer to lithium vacancies in lithium layer, ending up with a spinel structure. Besides, ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulation result manifests that oxygen release at the end of charging also exacerbates the Mn migration [51]. This is a good example of cation disordering being associated with phase instability upon lithium removal or uptake.

TM migrations can also happen during surface reconstruction. For Ni-rich NMCs, Ni tends to migrate from TM layer to octahedral sites in Li layer either through tetrahedral interstices or oxygen vacancies [52] after lithium removal which reduces coulomb repulsion to the migrated TM [53]. This migration causes the Li-Ni mixing in the bulk and irreversible spinel phase and/or rock-salt NiO phase transformation on the surface, for which the ratio of spinel phase and rock-salt phase varies with specific material composition and test conditions. For example, Jung et al. [16]. observed dominating spinel phase with little NiO rock-salt phase on the surface of LiNi0.5Co0.2Mn0.3O2 when cycled from 3 to 4.5 V after 50 cycles, but spinel phase enclosed by rock-salt when cycled between 3 and 4.8 V as it is illustrated in Fig. 4a. The difference between bulk and surface transformation is probably attributed to severer surface oxygen release, which generates more Ni migration pathways, thus larger extent of nickel migration [54]. The rock-salt structure formed on the surface hinders Li transportation and restricts deep delithiation in the bulk due to interfacial lattice strain between the reconstructed surface and the bulk layered structure, causing bulk fatigue of NMC811[55]. Compared with octahedral to octahedral migration, partially reversible Ni migration from octahedral to tetrahedral sites in Li layer also happens in LiNiO2 [56]. Although the Ni ions on tetrahedra sites impacts Li diffusion during cycling, they are beneficial for structural stability due to its suppression on lattice gliding, which prevents O3 to O1 phased transformation in LiNiO2 [56,57]. What is worth mentioning, both the octahedral-site Ni and tetrahedral-site Ni in Li layer are beneficial for structure stability owing to their “pillar effect”[52–57].

The slow cation migration of Ni and Mn during cycling causes gradual phase transformation starting from surface and propagating to the bulk. However, there are some transition metals that can migrate very fast, such as Mo and Cr ions in Li1.211Mo0.467Cr0.3O2, leading to high cation mixing. Li1.211Mo0.467Cr0.3O2 synthesized through standard solid-state procedures forms as a layered structure but transforms to a disordered rock-salt structure after just a few charge-discharge cycles (Fig. 4b). Via Rietveld refinement, it is estimated that 34–52 % of the TM ions migrated to Li layers after 10 cycles, indicating a severe cation mixing. The reversible capacity of the transformed material was 265.6 mA h/g at C/20 rate (16.4 mA/ g), even higher than that of the pristine structure, as further rationalized by a percolation theory featuring a three dimensional Li transport network [23]. This opened up a new avenue for researchers to explore in the study of DRX compounds.

Although TM migration is common in Li-rich oxides, it has been rarely observed in lithium rich sulfides although both of them involve anionic redox for charge compensation, where anionic redox is known for causing TM migration, as represented by Li1.33 – 2y/3Ti0.67 – y/3Fe y S2 [61]. This could be attributed to the higher covalency between TM and sulfur anions that can flexibly adapt the local environment of TMS6, thereby endowing higher stability of TMs inside the distorted lattice. However, this awaits further validation via a thorough study. As for sodium ion cathodes, TM migration should be eliminated due to the larger ionic radius difference between Na+ and TM ions. However, this is not the case. Partially reversible TM migration to Na+ layer is also observed [62]. Some researchers indicate that TM migration to Li+ or Na+ layer maybe the origin of lattice contraction in c axis in late charging stage of both lithium and sodium layered cathodes due to its smaller radius [57,62]. Nevertheless, c axis still follows ”expansion to contraction” pattern during charging in lithium sulfide (Li1.33 – 2y/3Ti0.67 – y/3Fe y S2), where there is no TM migration, challenging this hypothesis [61].

Intralayer TM migration, just like interlayer TM migration, also causes cation disordering within the TM layer. Intralayer TM migration in Li1.2Ni0.13Co0.13Mn0.54O2 causes the loss of honeycomb ordering and clustering of TM vacancies in TM layer, which is assumed to traps O2 molecules and is deemed as one candidate origin of first-cycle voltage hysteresis in Li-rich NMCs (Fig. 4c) [59]. Similar cases are also reported in sodium ion cathodes [60,63]. House et al. [60]. found that Na0.75[Li0.25Mn0.75]O2 with honeycomb superstructure suffers from TM migration and concomitant voltage hysteresis during first charge. In contrast, the stable ribbon superstructure in Na0.6[Li0.2Mn0.8]O2 is free of TM migration within TM layer, thus mitigating voltage hysteresis (Fig. 4d).

The transition from cation order to disorder in layered structure is the most common scenario, while the reversed case (disorder-to-order transition) can be seldom observed since it is normally an entropy decrease process and thus not thermodynamically favored. However, in some cases disorder to order transition can happen owing to Li-driven TM migrations in disordered rock-salt compounds. Fe ion in cation-disordered Li1.17Ti0.33Fe0.5O2 has been demonstrated to migrate from octahedral site to tetrahedral site during cycling accompanied by an extra ordering violating the parent face-centered unit cell as evidenced by sharp spots in the [58] ED pattern of the charged material. Such migrations were shown partially reversible during the first cycle and can be highly reversible during the subsequent cycling. The additional ordering in the square center of rock-salt unit cells quite resembles the ordering from spinel structures [64]. The Mn migration in disordered Li and Mn-rich Li1+xMn1−3xTi2xO2 (x = 0.05, 0.1, and 0.15) leads to the formation of new phase, referred to as δ phase. This new phase shows partial spinel-like ordering with a limited coherence length. In the pristine DRX Li1+xMn1−3xTi2xO2 (x = 0.05, 0.1 and 0.15), cations are equally and randomly distributed on 16c and 16d sites. After cycling, Mn ions rearrange towards 16d sites (spinel like order) due to high mobility of Mn cations. However, the remaining Ti ions are immobile thus preventing long range spinel order and consequent detrimental 3 V plateau in fully ordered spinel cathode. This δ phase displays exceptional energy density（826 Wh•kg−1） and cyclability [65]. Similarly, it is also revealed that partial fluorine (F) substituted Li and Mn-rich Li1.2Ti0.4–xMn0.4+xO2.0-xFx (x=0 and 0.2) and Li1.2Mn0.7Nb0.1O1.8F0.2 also showed a DRX to spinel-like structure transition during cycling, which is also demonstrated to cause increased capacity [66,67].

In contrast to the concept of long-range order, short-range-order (SRO) describes the chemical ordering of atoms arranged in a crystal lattice but in a small proximity or a limited spatial range. It generally refers to atomic order over scales comparable to interatomic distances within a structure that at least has two kinds of atoms occupying at one crystallographic position. The formation of such SRO is driven by the lower interaction energies between some atomic pairs than others, hence resulting in a structure within which certain number of ordered low-energy-configurations exist rather than completely random occupation of the atoms. SRO has been intensively investigated in the area of metal alloys [68–71]. In battery materials, SRO was indeed referred in some early electrode materials such as LiNi0.5Mn0.5O2 as well as carbon anodes. The recently rising DRX cathodes are especially considered to have SRO that highly impacts their Li transport. In this section, we aim to briefly review the SRO in batteries to emphasize its important role in governing the performance of Li-ion batteries.

In layered oxide cathodes, long-range cation order is predominant while SRO also exists especially within the transition metal layer when multiple TM ions are present. This SRO appears only when TM ions form local ordering inside transition metal layer out of the layered ordering. Transition metal arrangement in TM layers was mysterious at the beginning due to the difficulty in distinguishing TM ion occupation by normal methods based on X-ray diffraction [72,73]. This is not any longer true when advanced techniques like neutron pair distribution function (PDF) and NMR are used to resolve the local environment of lithium and transition metal ions. LiNi0.5Mn0.5O2, as a classical layered electrode material for Li-ion batteries, adopts the same hexagonal closed-packing oxygen framework alike LiCoO2 layered structure. It was generally believed that Ni, Mn and a small amount of Li ions (due to almost 10 % Ni/Li site exchange) are randomly distributed in transition metal layer. However, via a comprehensive investigation with a joint X-ray and neutron diffraction, PDF and NMR techniques, Grey et al. demonstrated that short-range order exists within TM layers. Both the Ni and Li atoms are, on average, tend to be close to more Mn ions compared with random distribution in the transition metal layers. They form clusters of LiMn6 and LiMn5Ni with specific Ni and Mn contacts that resemble the pattern observed in the "flower structure" or structures derived from ordered honeycomb arrays [74]. Similarly, It is demonstrated by Yang et al. [75]. that LiMn6 unit also exists in TM layer of Mn-based Li-rich layered oxides (Mn-LLOs). Moreover, it is also shown that diffraction techniques can also be used to pinpoint the local cation ordering in TM layer of NMC111 by refinement of experimental data through FAULTS program as demonstrated by Reynaud et al. [76].

Unlike layered oxides, DRX are more prone to have SRO as multiple cations share one crystallographic site. SRO structure in DRX materials is normally a convolution of three basic types of cation clusters: tetrahedron, octahedron, and cube in the FCC cation sublattice ( Fig. 5, “Structure” panel) [77]. These structures could be reflected by diffuse scattering in electron diffraction (ED) pattern and fast Fourier transform (FFT) of scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) (Fig. 5, “Characterization” panel) [77]. Notably, they can only observe point defects rather than quantify them in space. Besides, SRO can also be obtained through simulation, such as Monte Carlo, reverse Monte Carlo (Fig. 5, “Calculation panel) [78]. SRO among DRX materials consistently deteriorate their electrochemical performance via breakdown of the Li percolation network, which highly inhibits the Li transport, when compared to fully random cation distributions [4,77,79]. This has been demonstrated by Ceder et al. [4]. in two archetypical Li-rich cation-disordered compounds, Li1.2Ti0.4Mn0.4O2 and Li1.2Zr0.4Mn0.4O2, with the former having an SRO featuring octahedral cation cluster while the latter characteristic of tetrahedral cation cluster. They further proved that, facilitated by Monte Carlo method, the SRO of Li1.2Ti0.4Mn0.4O2 is better than that of Li1.2Zr0.4Mn0.4O2 to have well connected 0-TM (the one has the lowest migration barrier) Li diffusion pathways which enables percolation of Li ions (Fig. 5, “Property” panel). This pioneering work opens a new avenue for researcher to understand and optimize the electrochemical performance of DRX compounds by controlling the SRO.

How to engineer the SRO in DRX compounds is crucial for their further performance improvement. Ceder’s group also proposed that there are mainly two factors that can affect the characteristic of SRO: charge and size effect of the cations. In DRX material, high-valent transition metals (TMs) such as Mn3+, Ti4+, and Nb5+ are prone to repel each other and disperse as homogeneous as possible around Li+ ions to maintain local electroneutrality. This will effectively hinder the segregation of Li+ ions to form local Li4 tetrahedra clusters which have been shown crucial for forming 0-TM diffusion pathway. This may suggest metals with lower valence, such as Ni2+, are more preferred in forming desired SRO with better Li mobility, but it also depends on other specific factors such as the redox capacity of doping cations. On the other hand, the size mismatch between the high-valent TMs and Li+ ions promote Li+ segregation to minimize strain within the structure. Therefore, the coupling of these charge and size effects determines what kind of SRO will exist in a cation-disordered structure.

Since SRO is highly related with the intrinsic nature of the cations, the way to effectively control SRO in DRX should be to choose the right cations in constructing the compounds. However, cations that can form desired SRO are probably not those that can facilitate high capacities since electrochemical performances are holistically dependent on various factors. Hence, it calls for other means to regulate SRO without limiting the kinds of cationic species. Indeed, three methods, including cation and anion substitution, designing high-entropy compounds, and playing with annealing parameters are potential to realize the SRO engineering. Replacing the low-valence cations with high-valence cations can unavoidably drives the lithium clustering together at the vicinity of high-valence cations to maintain local charge neutrality, hence will largely alter the SRO. Fluorine substitution tunes SRO in DRX material as well. Substituting fluorine for oxygen has been widely used to increase the capacity of DRX material because it triggers lower oxidation state of transition metal ion [80–83]. However, transition metal ions preferably bond with oxygens due to their higher covalency while Li tends to bond with F ions due to the high ionicity. This creates local transition metal-oxygen-rich regions and lithium-fluorine-rich regions, hence forming additional SRO. Although more fluorine creates more lithium rich regions and correspondingly high lithium diffusivity, ultrahigh fluorine content is restricted by its synthesis method [84]. Another strategy is to develop high-entropy DRX compounds, which encompasses a variety of cation species in DRX structure, where the randomness of cation occupation can largely suppress the SRO. Lun et al. [85]. have successfully rationalized the synthesis of a group of DRX cathodes with two, four, or six transition metal (TM) species, observing a consistent decrease in short-range order and a systematic increase in energy density and rate capability as more TM cation species are introduced, as the suppressed SRO is shown beneficial for lithium transport. Controlling annealing parameters is another strategy to engineer SRO. Li et al. [77]. successfully altered SRO structure of Li1.2Ti0.4Mn0.4O2.0 (LTMO) from octahedral cluster to cubic cluster by annealing at 500 °C for 60 h, which is demonstrated by electron diffraction pattern. This transition increased specific capacity and proved once again the significant effect of SRO structure on electrochemical performance of cathode material. Besides, quenching has been early shown as a powerful method in controlling SRO in metal alloys [69,86,87]. It was introduced to cathode material field as well to tune the electrochemical performance of cathode material either by increasing proportion of Li2MnO3 phase in Li-rich NMCs or tap density in LiFePO4/C[88–90]. But evidences of showing SRO controlling by quench method in cathode material are scarce.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The roles of cation order and disorder in cathode materials

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium diffusion in solid materials is crucial in determining the capacity and rate capability of cathode material for Li-ion batteries. Apparent lithium diffusivity can be always obtained by galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) method and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement from a macroscopic perspective. Self-diffusion coefficient is evaluated through calculating the activation energy of Li+ hopping using DFT method from microscopic perspective [18]. In rock-salt type materials, both Li and TM ions occupy octahedral (o) sites. Li+ ions hop between two octahedral (o) sites via a tetrahedral (t) interstice (o-t-o diffusion mechanism, shown in Fig. 6a). At least two octahedral Li sites must be connected through the activated tetrahedral site to achieve Li+ diffusion. Four cations in octahedral sites are face-sharing with a tetrahedral site and form a tetrahedral cluster. The energy barrier for Li diffusion is related to the energy of sitting Li at such an intermediate tetrahedral site, which is also known as a transition state for Li o-to-o migration. The energy barrier is highly dependent on two factors: (1) the four cationic species around the tetrahedral site; (2) the height of the tetrahedron. In this sense, cation order and disorder play a significant role on the Li diffusivity as it affects the cationic compositions and the height of the tetrahedrons.

The valence of the face-sharing TM ions is the first crucial parameter in determining the diffusion barrier, as higher valence generates stronger coulombic interaction (Fig. 6b). Therefore, the Li diffusivity will be higher when the four cationic species are vacancies or Li ions than when they are high-valence transition metals. This answers the reason why 0-TM and 1-TM channels are more diffusion-favorable than 3-TM and 4-TM channels, the latter being diffusion-inhibiting due to the very large energy barrier caused by large Columbic repulsions [18,91]. Cation order and disorder will highly impact the Li diffusivity in rock-salt oxides as it determines how many TM ions present around an intermediate tetrahedral site. In layered oxides, there are normally three Li+/vacancies and one TM ion around a tetrahedral site in Li layer and hence low energy barrier of diffusion can be expected. However, once interlayer cation mixing happens, the diffusion barrier can be increased due to more TM ions around the tetrahedral site. On the other hand, the available space for relaxation [18], which is determined by the thickness of the Li layer (higher Li slab distance means higher tetrahedron height and hence lower energy barrier), is also determinative to Li diffusivity. In Ni-based layered materials, a fraction of Ni (usually within 10 %) always exchanges with Li sites during synthesis, known as Li-Ni mixing. This significantly decreases Li slab thickness and thereby increases Li+ migration barrier. In DRX material, smaller tetrahedron height of 1-TM channels caused by cation mixing restricted Li diffusion through this kind of channel. Thus, Li+ in DRX material mainly hops through active 0-TM channel rather than 1-TM channel due to its smaller tetrahedron height, although there are less 0-TM channels than 1-TM channels. However, an isolated 0-TM channel is not enough for long-range lithium diffusion. There must be enough 0-TM channels percolating to form a connected network that allows long range Li+ diffusion (Fig. 6c and d). This requires over 9 % lithium excess (x>1.09), as evidenced by Ceder’s group, for fully disordered LixTM2−xO2 to make 0-TM channel percolate (Fig. 6e) [23]. This threshold may vary with different systems due to the existence of short-range order (SRO) [4,93,94]. Besides, nano-sized DRX material is not restricted by this threshold due to sufficiently short lithium transport pathway [95,96].

Cation order, as represented in typical layered compounds, separates the lithium and transition metal in different layers and is therefore beneficial for Li diffusivity and reversibility. However, in DRX where cation disorder presents, the reversibility of Li (de)intercalation is not good, devastating their cycling performance. For example, most Li-rich layered materials retain over 80 % of their initial capacity after 100 cycles [49,64,97,98], while DRX materials normally lose over 20 percent of their initial capacity within 20th to 30th cycles [64,99,100,58,101] ( Fig. 7a and b). This questions whether cation disorder is imperative in determining the cyclability of cathode materials.

For Li-rich layered material, material degradation has been well elucidated by several studies [103]. Anionic redox brings about severe oxygen release on particle surface. The oxygen release facilitates TM migration, generating a densified layer near surface and severely exacerbated capacity decay due to its restriction on lithium diffusion [104–107]. Besides, partial reversible migration of TM during charging and discharging causes phase transition on material surface and is consistently recognized to be coupled with capacity decay [108–110]. Although cation disorder generated by TM migration to Li layer during cycling is always detrimental to electrochemical performance of Li-rich layered materials, Li-TM disorder among TM layer, denoted as intralayer disorder, can be leveraged to tailor the electrochemical performance. Song et al. [97]. reported that more superior cyclability can be achieved by introducing intralayer Li-Mn disorder in TM layer of Li0.700[Li0.222Mn0.756]O2 (
R
3
̅
m
space group) that has mitigated Jahn–Teller distortion compared with those having honeycomb ordered Li-Mn in TM layer as represented by Li[Li1/3Mn2/3]O2 (C2/m space group). The stable structure with
R
3
̅
m
space group is further evidenced by highly reversible structural evolution during first cycle and second charge process as it is verified by in situ XRD and ex situ extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) spectra (Fig. 7c). Similarly, intralayer Li-Ru disorder in TM layer of O3 type Li2RuO3 [102] (Fig. 7d) and Li0.6Li0.2Ru0.8O2 [111] also plays a paramount role in improving cycling performance by either preventing O-O dimerization (former) or irreversible TM migration to alkali layer (later) [102,111].

The cases of degradation mechanisms in DRX compounds are similarly reported. By studying Li1.3Nb0.3Mn0.4O2 cycled within different voltage ranges, Chen et al. found that anionic redox induced deep reduction of TM ion, leading to lower redox potential and voltage fade in DRX material [112]. Similar to Li-rich layered material, densified layer along with oxygen release is also a detrimental factor that causes capacity degradation for DRX material [113]. As it is discussed above, lithium ion diffusion in DRX material relies on the 0-TM channel percolation, which strongly depends on Li-excess. The densified layer has very low Li/TM ratio, restricting lithium diffusion and thus leading to capacity loss [47,112]. This is a major factor that causes severe polarization of some Ni-based DRX materials, such as Li–Ni–Ti–Mo oxides [47], Li-Ni-Ti-O compounds [114]. Indeed, Ceder et al. [47]. discovered that there is almost 39 % oxygen loss after 20 cycles of Li1.2Ni1/3Ti1/3Mo2/15O2 with DRX configuration. Such severe oxygen release contributes largely to the poorer cyclability in DRX material compared with lithium-rich layered materials [115]. Based on the calculation from Seo et al. [116]. oxygen around d0 transition metals are easier to bond together due to less directional bonding compared with that around redox active transition metals. This provides us a clue that d0-TM based DRX compounds have less covalent TM-O bond and therefore is more susceptible to oxygen release. Hence, two routes can be envisaged to mitigate or even eliminate oxygen release in DRX. First one is to decrease the participation of anionic redox by either high-valence d0 metal doping [117,118] or low valence F- substitution for O2- [119–121]. They both decrease valence of redox active TM, thus increasing cationic redox contribution. Second strategy is to increase the covalency between transition metal and oxygen, which stabilizes oxygen in the lattice [117,122]. However, in some cases of DRX cathodes where F substitutes O anions, anionic redox is much suppressed due to the increase of cationic redox contribution, while the cycling stability is still far from significantly improved. Therefore, more work should be implemented on uncovering the real electrochemical and chemical origins of the degradation of DRX materials. One thread of such study should be to decouple the factors from surface electrode-electrolyte interaction and the bulk materials failure. This could be realized by surface modification of DRX to weaken or even eliminate the surface contribution on performance degradation, thereby unveiling the effect of bulk structural change. One possible bulk origin of capacity fading in DRX could be that, upon deep delithiation, the formation of Li vacancies, sometimes even clustering together, will drive cation migrations, leading to the evolution of SRO and the loss of Li percolating network. However, capturing such cation rearrangement is challenging in disordered rock-salt cathodes due to the absence of cation order at the beginning. SRO, however, may offer a direction for us to explore the structural change as one can easily collect the information of SRO by electron diffraction or PDF.

All in all, although DRX materials suffer from poorer cyclability compared with layered material, it is still hard to tell whether this poor cyclability originates solely from the cation disorder structure or the special chemical compositions of DRX material, or even both. Because most DRX materials are based on d0 TM, whose bonding with O atoms are more ionic compared with other TMs, such as Ni, Co, and more likely to cause irreversible oxygen release. Besides, intralayer disorder and interlayer disorder of layered cathodes lead to opposite effects on cyclability, probably due to impeded Li diffusion and stress following phase transition caused by TM in Li layer.

Voltage profile in voltage-specific capacity plot plays a vital role in evaluating inner structure evolution of cathode material during charging and state of charge (SOC) prediction in battery management system (BMS) [123]. Equilibrium potential of cathode is negatively proportional to Li chemical potential (
eU ∝ −
μ
Li
, where
μ
Li
is the chemical potential summation of Li+ and an electron, namely,
μ
Li
=
μ
Li
+
+
μ
e
−
). Thus, charge/discharge potential is determined by both lithium site energy and electronic structure [124]. On one hand, compared with ordered structure like layered oxides, cation disorder will apparently lead to disparate cation environments around Li+, contributing to different lithium site energies. On the other hand, cation disordering also leads to the reshuffling of electronic structures, typical examples being the O 2p non-bonding states aroused by Li-O-Li configurations engendered by cation disordering. The energy level of the O 2p non-bonding states is also dependent on the cationic species around the O ions; the more the TM ions and the higher the valence of TM ions, the lower the energy of O 2p non-bonding levels due to Coulombic interactions. Therefore, DRX materials, which are cation disordered, should have larger variations in both
μ
Li
+
and
μ
e
−
compared with its layered counterparts due to its nearly random distribution of cations [95]. This normally results in a voltage slope rather than a voltage plateau during charging in most of the DRX materials, as evidenced in numerous publications [41,47,66,67,95,101,125].

Besides, Li-driven phase transition also affects the potential curve, in a manner that biphasic transition generally leads to a plateau whereas solid-solution reaction generally leads to a slope [126]. For layered structures, lithium-driven reaction normally results in phase transitions due to the layer gliding. This can be typically observed in LiNiO2 compound where its electrochemical curve consists of several voltage plateaus. Unlike layered structure, DRX compounds are free of layer-gliding phase transitions due to the absence of physical layers as Li and TM are intermixed together; in some extent, one can consider that the layer gliding is inhibited in DRX due to the “pillar” effect of TM in lithium layer. DRX electrodes normally proceed the reaction through a solid-solution phase transition [7,84,85,127,128,129] and therefore are more likely to have a voltage slope. The effect of cation disordering on voltage profile can be more clearly observed through the staircase-to-slope curve transition during the first cycle of Li-rich layered compounds, such as Li-rich NMCs and Li2RuO3-based compounds [49,130,131]. The cation rearrangement during the first charging process generates various Li environments and O electronic structures, hence leading to slope curve during discharging. This can be further verified by recent findings that honeycomb superstructure is more susceptible to cation migration, hence leading to large first-cycle voltage curve evolution (they termed as first-cycle voltage hysteresis), whereas ribbon and mesh superstructures are more robust and hence is free of curve evolution [60].

Power density of lithium-ion batteries heavily relies on average working voltage. Saubanère et al. [132]. proposed that cation disorder renders higher average potential compared with ordered homologues by implying a simplified voltage calculation formulation. DFT calculation results from Aydinol’s group further support this conclusion. DRX Li1−xTMO2 (TM = V, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni) forms a longer average TM–O bond length than layered Li1−xTMO2. This is the result of “pillar effect” caused by larger ion radius of Li+ located in smaller TM ion position [133]. The longer TM-O bond means higher ionicity of this bond and lower the energy level of anti-bonding TM d state, which causes higher average voltage of DRX. Abdellahi et al. argued that the influence of cation disorder on average voltage varies with chemical species [134]. According to their DFT calculation result, V and Cr lead to a lower voltage upon cation disorder, while others are more likely to increase average voltage upon disorder in LiMO2 stoichiometry (M = Ti, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni). However, experimental result runs counter to this conclusion. LiCoO2 synthesized under low temperature (400℃) with 6 % cobalt within the lithium layers showed a lower average discharge voltage compared with that of perfectly layered LiCoO2 (synthesized under 900℃) counterpart [135]. This may be due to the impact of kinetics, such as polarization, that resulted from different experimental conditions, where DFT calculation did not consider. In such cases, one should use GITT method to unravel the thermodynamic equilibrium potential to determine the potential difference between different electrodes with the presence of cation order or disorder

Anionic redox in some lithium rich layered materials, such as Li2TiO3 [8,136], Li2TiS3 [8], Li2ZrO3 [8] are electrochemical inactive. Such inactivity has remained hard to understand at the beginning since it is not directly related to electronic conductivity, as Li2TiS3, which was assumed to be electronic conductive (black powder with very small bandgap as inferred from DFT calculation), remains electrochemically inactive [137]. To explore why anionic redox is limited in these systems, Li et al. [3,138]. have taken a study by incorporating Fe in the lattice of electrochemically inactive cation-disorder Li2TiO3, triggering substantial oxygen redox. Via a systematical spectroscopic and theoretical investigations, they found the activation of oxygen redox in Li-Ti-Fe-O system is nested at the catalytic effect of Fe that alters the electron transfer pathway by which oxygen redox can happen [138]. Such finding was also extended to sulfide systems where sulfur redox in inactive layered Li2TiS3 can be largely activated through the incorporation of Co[139,140]. Following studies have reproduced the same rationale of anionic redox activation via substituting Fe2+, Ti3+, and even Se2- into Li2TiS3 [139,141–143].

Interestingly, while both layered and cation-disordered Li2TiO3 is suffering from inactivity, cation-disordered chalcogenides, such as Li2TiS3 [144] and Li3NbS4 [136], can have higher anionic redox activities, despite that their ordered counterparts are inactive. A deep exploration of the case of Li3NbS4 has unraveled that ordered Li3NbS4 consists of weakly interacting clusters that can be considered electronically isolated from each other in the condensed phase, which should be responsible for its inactivity. In contrast, disordered Li3NbS4 shows disordered arrangement of the Nb and Li occupation, thereby breaking the isolation of Nb4S16 cluster, hence having quite dispersive bands and a very small band gap, indicative of significant inter-cluster covalent interactions [136]. This explains well why in disordered Li3NbS4 system the anionic redox activity can be well activated. Nevertheless, the reason why the electrochemical activity of Li2TiO3, even with disordering, can never be activated could be due to their large ionicity that remains higher than sulfides even by cation disordering. Given that there are still some unanswered questions, more work should be designed to revisit this underlying mechanism of redox activation in the future, especially regarding how to utilize the cation disordering to engineer anionic redox activity.

Voltage hysteresis is voltage gap between charging and discharging curve, which always causes severe energy loss and heat release. It consists of various contributions from different events, such as ohmic drop, lithium diffusion, electrochemical reactions etc., which have different time scales. Kinetic origin of voltage hysteresis, such as polarization, is eliminable by cycling under slow rate or galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) test. However, huge voltage hysteresis persists even under extremely slow cycle rate under GITT test [2], which leads researchers to focus on the investigation of their thermodynamic origins [145,146].

Cation disordering, such as induced by TM migration during charging, is deemed as a significant candidate that causes voltage hysteresis. House et al. [59]. consider that oxygen redox produces O2 molecule, which is trapped in the bulk particle during charging, accompanied by TM migration. When O2 is reduced back to lattice during discharging, Li ion takes the site occupied by TM before charging. This results in O2- fully coordinated by Li ions and O 2p states located in a higher level, which caused voltage hysteresis. By DFT calculation, Gent et al. [17]. successfully captured O 2p orbital energy increase after Mn and Ni migration in delithiated LR-NMC (As it is shown in Fig. 8a). They also demonstrated the strong correlation between TM migration and voltage hysteresis by XRD data refinement and further demonstrated the close connection between TM migration and voltage hysteresis (shown in Fig. 8b). This is further verified by smaller voltage hysteresis of Na0.6[Li0.2Mn0.8]O2 with ribbon-ordered TM layer compared with that of honeycomb-ordered Na0.75[Li0.25Mn0.75]O2 due to less TM migration [60].

Interestingly, while cation migration is believed to be the cause of voltage hysteresis, DRX, which is already a cation disordered structure, have even larger voltage hysteresis than layered structures (Fig. 8d and e). This leads to a legitimate question regards whether cation disorder is the origin of voltage hysteresis. However, the possibility was negated by a study on a DRX compound, Li1.17Ti0.33Fe0.5O2, which enlists large voltage hysteresis during O redox but very small hysteresis during Fe redox [2], highlighting that voltage hysteresis is independent of cation ordering but dependent on redox mechanism. Ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT), which describes a non-equilibrium pathway of electron transfer, is further proven to be related to the huge voltage hysteresis, characteristic of initial overoxidation of transition metal followed by the electron transfer from O2- to transition metal ion (Fig. 8c). Such LMCT theory provides a clue for us to explain why the voltage hysteresis in DRX compounds is generally larger than those in layered compounds. DRX compounds, all based on d0-TM ions, have relatively lower covalency compared with those in layered systems, such as Li2MnO3 and Li2RuO3 systems. The ionicity in DRX compounds requires larger energy barrier to overcome once anionic redox generates local structural distortions, such as O-O dimerization and cation migration, hence require higher overpotential for the reaction to happen. Therefore, in this extent, voltage hysteresis is independent of the cation ordering while the case in DRX is correlated with their electronic structure from d0-TM ions.

Apart from electrochemical performance, safety performance of lithium ion batteries is also closely related to cation order and disorder of cathodes. Small amount of Li-Ni disorder in NMCs may have an impact on their thermal stability. It is demonstrated by Li et al. [19]. that 5.7 % Li-Ni disorder induced by F substitution increases onset decomposition temperature of LiNi0.85Co0.075Mn0.075O2 by 48℃ and activates superior cyclability under 55 ℃. However, moderate cation disorder ratio is prerequisite for layered cathodes since excessive cation disorder induces phase transformation from layered to spinal or rocksalt, hampering capacity releasing. Besides, anionic redox, which is always concomitant with TM migration during charging in layered cathodes, induces severe oxygen release and consecutive exothermic side reactions with electrolytes [152,153]. Thus, anionic redox may cause severe safety concerns although they provide extra capacities for cathodes in lithium-ion batteries. Beyond that, heat release caused by voltage hysteresis is another high-risk hazard that may induce thermal runaway. TM migration induced cation disorder in layered cathodes is always deemed as the origin of voltage hysteresis [60]. Besides, globally cation disordered rocksalt (DRX) materials exhibit larger voltage hysteresis than layered materials (Discussed in detail in section: “Voltage hysteresis”.), further limiting massive implementation of DRX compounds [47,99,147,149].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controlling and characterizing cation order and disorder
ce:section.ce:para: As cation order and disorder play a pivotal role in governing the electrochemical performance of cathode materials, how to manipulate the cation ordering by experimental approaches is of paramount importance. In this section, we summarize diverse experimental methodologies in controlling cation order and disorder within cathode materials, including tuning Li-Ni mixing in NMC materials, synthesis of novel DRX compounds and some metastable structures obtained by ionic exchange method. Besides, it remains equally important to review characterization techniques, as we have done next, to detect the cation order or disorder inside cathode materials in different geometric scales. These include X-ray diffraction (XRD), high angle angular dark field-scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM), electron diffraction (ED), neutron diffraction coupled with reverse Monte Carlo (RMC) simulation, pair distribution function (PDF), and so on.

As discussed at the beginning, Ni2+ is prone to generate Li-Ni mixing due to its similar radius to Li+ in Ni-rich layered material, which severely affects electrochemical performance of Ni-containing cathodes. Controlling Li-Ni mixing precisely during synthesis is the prerequisite of better electrochemical performance for Ni-containing layered cathodes.

From the point view of chemical composition, Mn4+ can aggravates Li-Ni mixing by generating more Ni2+ through charge compensation and also magnetic frustration that can only be relieved via Li+/Ni2+ mixing, whereas Co3+helps inhibit Li-Ni mixing by stabilizing the magnetic interactions [25]. These considerations have been validated by numerous NMC cathodes and hint a vast space of chemical compositional design of cathode material to control Li-Ni mixing ( Fig. 9a) [24–26]. Besides, synthetic conditions also play a paramount role in controlling Li-Ni cation mixing. Cui et al. [36]. uncovered that sol–gel method synthesized NCM811 possesses lower Li-Ni mixing compared with that made by hydrothermal method due to more homogenous cation distribution in sol–gel process, thus better cyclability (Fig. 9b). In addition, solid-state annealing parameter is critical as well. Sun et al. revealed that as the holding time increases at 500 ℃ after calcination, cation mixing decreases linearly[34]. Orlova et al. reported that different annealing atmospheres resulted in different kinds of Li-Ni mixing schemes in LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2——annealing in air results in Ni for Li substitution in lithium layer, whereas annealing under oxygen flow generally leads to Li-Ni anti-site disorder since
Ni
Ni
•
defects (Ni3+ taking Ni2+ site) generated under oxygen flow is coupled with more
Li
Ni
′
(Li+ taking Ni2+ site) generation to maintain charge neutrality [154]. However, the impact on electrochemical performance of these two Li-Ni mixing schemes still needs to be investigated. Lastly, ion exchange as a method to prevent the happening of cation mixing is demonstrated very helpful. Li(Ni0.5Mn0.5)O2 obtained from Na(Ni0.5Mn0.5)O2 by ion exchange method, for example, well preserves the layered structure with minor Li-Ni mixing and exhibits superior rate capability compared with that obtained from traditional solid state method (Fig. 9c) [91].

DRX cathode is a class of exotic compounds showing a unique percolating Li diffusion mechanism differing from that of layered ones. Its high capacity exceeding traditional layered cathodes and low cost due to cobalt-free composition makes it a highly promising cathode for lithium ion batteries [156]. However, its synthesis remains indeed one of the big challenges towards practical implementation. Successful DRX material synthesis is usually performed on the basis of d0 transition metal ions as they favor the structural distortion induced by cation disorder. Commonly used methods for synthesizing DRX include ball-milling [4,125], sol-gel [3,117], and co-precipitation [2], followed by a high-temperature ceramic process to facilitate the formation of cation-disordered structures. There are also some DRX materials formed solely by high energy ball-milling without any calcination process [157], which leads us to suspect that some DRX coumpounds may not be thermodynamically stable, but only kinetically stable. Ball-milling is the most prevalent treatment used in DRX synthesis either to homogenize the precursors for solid-state annealing or to crush large particles into smaller ones thereby enabling short Li+ diffusion pathways, or even to facilitate the contact of carbon with the active materials. The use of ball-milling is essential to activate the electrochemical performance of most of the DRX compounds as they normally form large-sized bulk materials that impede the Li diffusivity in cation-disordered network, unless sol-gel method, as shown in some cases, can be utilized to obtain nano-sized particles. However, ball-milling will inevitably destroy the materials crystallinity and create defects that are not good to the chemical and electrochemical stability of the materials. Therefore, the practicality of such DRX cathodes is highly limited by the essentiality of using ball-milling method, hence more superior methods should be explored in the future to directly obtain smaller particles that can sustain Li percolations. On the other hand, other unusual methods were also reported to prepare DRX compounds. A novel microwave assisted solid state method was proposed by Wu et al. [129]. which shortened reaction time to 5 minutes. Li1.2Mn0.4Ti0.4O2 synthesized through this method exhibited initial discharge capacity of almost 200 mAh/g (Fig. 9d), which is similar to the same material obtained from traditional solid state method in their group. However, this capacity is lowered than the Li1.2Mn0.4Ti0.4O2 synthesized by similar solid state method coupled with ball-milling (260 mAh/g) in another group [4]. Thus, this method is still far from mass production for DRX cathodes. Strict synthesis conditions strongly hamper practical applications of these DRXcathodes together with their poor cycling stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metastable cation-order structure obtained by ion exchange method
ce:section.ce:para: Traditional solid-state high-temperature methods normally create thermodynamically stable structures. However, this leaves a large space of metastable materials behind that are less explored. These include some cation-ordered and -disordered materials that potentially exhibit intriguing electrochemical behaviors. Ion exchange method, characteristic of exchanging alkali ions in a stable oxygen framework host due to size-difference-induced driving force, makes it possible to synthesize materials with metastable cation-ordered structures, such as layered LiMnO2 or LiFeO2 which are thermodynamically-favored cation-disordered structures [48,91,158–161]. The precursors for ion exchange are normally those having larger-size alkaline cations, such as Na and K layered analogues, which are generally cation-ordered due to the more different sizes between alkaline and transition metal ions. Armstrong and Bruce [161] reported a metastable layered LiMnO2, which is an analogy of LiCoO2, by an typical ion exchange method, in which case α-NaMnO2 is refluxed with LiCl or LiBr solved in n-hexanol solution. Vitin and West [48] reproduced such layered LiMnO2 following their method and tested its electrochemical performance, suggesting that layered LiMnO2 obtained by ion-exchange method reveals unstable nature and undergoes a layered to spinel-related phase transition and severe capacity fade when cycled to a low voltage (1.5 V). Nevertheless, question still remains about metastable structure synthesis and suspicion of cycling stability of metastable structure. Apart from refluxing with lithium halide solution, molten salt ion exchange method, where lithium salt and precursor are mixed evenly and heated for a few hours, is also widely utilized to obtain thermodynamically metastable ordered structures due to its simple experimental installation [91,160,162–164]. Kang et al. [91]. utilized the molten salt ion exchange method to synthesis Li(Ni0.5Mn0.5)O2 with less Li-Ni mixing through perfectly layered Na(Ni0.5Mn0.5)O2 as precursor. By keeping minor Na-Ni mixing in Na(Ni0.5Mn0.5)O2, this Li(Ni0.5Mn0.5)O2 obtained by ion exchange method surmounted tricky Li-Ni mixing problem and displayed superior rate capability. Although this ion exchange has been widely utilized by researchers, its underlying mechanism is still elusive, making it difficult to control the ion exchange product. Han et al. [155]. discovered significant role of cation vacancy level of precursor and lithium preference in determining the feasibility of ion exchange. Lower vacancy level favors Li for Na substitution from a thermodynamic perspective (Fig. 9e), whereas it suffers from large kinetic barrier of ion exchange at the same time. Their findings shed light on exploring ion exchange mechanism although there is still a long way to go to make it clear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of cation order and disorder
ce:section.ce:para: The commonly used technique for detecting long-range cation ordering and disordering is diffraction, which includes X-ray, neutron and electron diffractions. Cation disordering in layered material, such as Li-Ni mixing in NMC material, is generally indicated by intensity ratio of (003) over (104) peaks in XRD ( Fig. 10a). The I(003)/I(104) intensity ratio is inversely proportional to the degree of cation disorder, namely, greater I(003)/I(104) intensity ratio means smaller degree of cation disorder since the cation mixing will average the scattering of Li and TM layer and thus weakens the layered spacing feature. A I(003)/I(104) ratio higher than 1.2 is normally the indicator of good layered material with negligible cation disorder [25,165,166], but it quite depends on the composition and specific cations involved. The (003) diffraction peak will fully disappear once the layered structure evolves to a totally cation-disorder structure (DRX material) [2,18], with probably some diffuse scattering intensity remained due to short-range ordering. However, the (003)/(104) intensity ratio remains as an empirical rule for identify the cation ordering, while more reliable analysis relies on Rietveld refinement of a well-resolved XRD pattern. Sample preparation for XRD analysis also matters as it also affects the peak intensities by means of, for example, introducing crystal preferential orientation. A more suitable choice would be collecting synchrotron XRD pattern for more quantitative analysis. Besides, Rietveld refinement of neutron diffraction pattern is also widely used to probe order and disorder quantitatively among cathode particles. For exaxmple, Zhao et al. [167]. successfully captured lower extent of Li-Ni mixng among NMC materials synthesised through co-precipitation (NMC(A)) compared with that obtained by solid-state reaction (NMC(B)) (Fig. 10c) by this method.

Different from XRD, HAADF-STEM can realize direct visualization of cation arrangement in atomic scale. TM ions has much higher Z contrast than Li ions through HAADF-STEM due to their higher atomic number, thereby enabling us to easily distinguish TM ion layer and Li layer in layered structure and cation ordering. Cation mixing can be easily captured by HAADF-STEM since it will arouse intensities in Li layer which is assumed to be transparent [2,3,18]. However, HAADF-STEM can hardly distinguish TM metals, such as Ni, Co, Mn etc. By combining HAADF-STEM with electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) with atomic resolution, we can identify the precise information of different TMs and tell which element is more easily to migrate and mixed with Li ions (Fig. 10d) [168,169].

On the other hand, detecting SRO remains a big challenge through normal diffraction method like XRD and neutron. However, electron diffraction（ED) can be a powerful technique to probe SRO [4,77,92]. SRO is reflected by diffuse scattering patterns around the Bragg reflections in ED pattern (Fig. 5, “Characterization” panel). The intensity and shape of diffuse scattering pattern reflect the strength and structure of short-range ordering respectively. Besides, Z-contrast image obtained from aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscopy（STEM）can also reflect SRO information after fast Fourier transform (FFT) (Fig. 5, “Characterization” panel) [77]. Coupling experimental approaches with theoretical ones is crucial in resolving the nature of SRO. By implying Cluster-expansion Monte Carlo simulations（CEMC), material structure can be reproduced and corresponding precise atomistic SRO structure is easily obtained [7,77]. Compared with this method, Reverse Monte Carlo (RMC) is more efficient in simulating super-large systems (up to tens of thousands of atoms) due to elimination of energy calculation. It is characterized by combining experimentally measured pair distribution function (PDF), denoted as G(r) here, with simulation algorithms (Fig. 5, “Calculation” panel) to obtain concrete local structure [7,170–172]. Through this RMC fitting result of G(r) data (Experimental PDF data is obtained by Neutron diffraction), Sun et al. [78]. successfully revealed SRO in Li1.16Ti0.37Ni0.37Nb0.10O2 and demonstrated pervasive site distortion of Ti4+-centric octahedron, which activates 1-TM channel among DRX (Fig. 10b).

Synthesis, structure, and performance have indivisible correlations and understanding their underlying mechanism helps direct next generation cathodes. In this review, we encapsulated general conceptualization of order and disorder in typical cathode materials and deciphered thermodynamic origins of the diversiform cation arrangements, including radius and charge, magnetic frustration and super-exchange effect and effect of d0 transition metal on stabilizing DRX material. Apart from order and disorder of as-synthesized cathodes, cation disorder generated during delithiation of layered cathodes is frequently observed, which causes phase transformation and concomitant voltage and capacity decay. Suppressing TM migration among layered materials are imperative in improving electrochemical performance of layered materials, either by increasing covalency between cation and anion or tuning superstructure among TM layers, or even enlarge the alkaline ion size. However, disorder to order transformation is not that perennial, probably due to the unfavored entropy decrease process. In addition, SRO of DRX materials and even for layered cathodes could potentially evolves during cycling, which causes performance degradation or enhancement that requires further exploration.

Further, we tried to deconvolute the roles of order and disorder on electrochemical performances among intricate effects, which include lithium diffusion, cyclability, average working potential, anionic redox activity, voltage profile and voltage hysteresis. Poor cyclability of DRX materials greatly weakens their competitiveness against ordered materials such as NMCs, Li-rich NMCs, etc. However, difficulty still exists in decoupling the effects of chemical composition and cationic arrangement on electrochemical performance. Besides, interlayer disorder and intralayer disorder in different systems play entirely different roles in governing the electrochemical behaviors in layered materials, owing to their different working mechanisms on lithium diffusion and phase transition. Nevertheless, it remains a long way for us to figure out why the cycling of DRX compounds, which have full-dimensional cation disorder, is quite limited towards practicality.

On the last aspect, controllable order and disorder engineering plays a paramount role in integrating theory and practice together for achieving better cathode design, the reason why we have reviewed this part is to pave the way for next-generation cathode design. This must be done by coupling state-of-the-art characterization techniques to well spot the cation order and disorder in diverse range of scales. We believe our review correlating the cation order and disorder with cathode electrochemistry through diverse range of aspects will be of value to the community for further cathode design.

As of now, efforts are still needed to be devoted to deconvoluting the role of cation arrangement in determining electrochemical performance for future cathode material reformation. For instance, intralayer disorder in Li-rich layered materials improves cyclability as evidenced by some works, whereas interlayer disorder generated during delithiation in both stoichiometric and Li-rich layered cathodes deteriorate cyclability under most circumstances. Besides, overall disordered DRX material are suffering from very poor cycling stability. This may also originate from the high ionicity of d0 metal, which aggravates oxygen release once oxygen redox presents. It will help if one can either obtain DRX material without d0 metal or layered material with d0 metal to decouple the role of TM-O covalency and cation order and disorder. Noteworthy, most TM migration will cause cation disorder and oxygen release during delithiation happening on particle surface of layered materials. Thus, it deserves to decouple the surface and bulk factors affecting the cycling stability by surface coating (to prevent surface degradation) or other methods. The interface generated between bulk phase and surface phase may also contribute to performance degradation, similar to tricky interface problem in all-solid-state batteries. Apart from cyclability, voltage hysteresis is another problem eagerly to be resolved to promote the implementation of high-capacity Li-rich layered cathodes especially considering the role of cation disorder inside. Attention should be paid on the covalency change during delithiation and decouple voltage hysteresis of anionic redox and cationic redox with the assistance of DFT and other simulation techniques. All in all, cation order and disorder are closely related to material performance although some underlying mechanisms are still unclear. Understanding these mechanisms should benefit designing of next generation cathodes and beyond.
Conceptualization, Biao Li, Yue Zhou; Writing – original draft, Yue Zhou, Biao Li; writing – review & editing, Biao Li and Jiaqiang Huang; Supervision, Biao Li and Jiaqiang Huang.
